Welcome back to Wall Power, Frieze Los Angeles edition. I=E2=80=99m Marion
Maneker.

Covering an art fair can be like an expedition, and I wanted to take you on=
 my weeklong journey here in L.A. So today I=E2=80=99m going to cram a lot =
of information about Frieze Los Angeles and the art market into one newslet=
ter. I also have a little more backstory on the pitch for the Riggio collec=
tion, now that Christie=E2=80=99s has won the consignment over Sotheby=E2=
=80=99s. So sit back and enjoy the ride=E2=80=A6

=E2=80=8A

A Frieze Day One Sales Report
-----------------------------

An art fair can focus a collector=E2=80=99s attention, so galleries use the=
 occasion to prime the pump,
as it were, sending their clients and art advisors, in the weeks beforehand=
, lists of the works that will be available at the fair. Many of the sales =
that are announced at Frieze L.A. are actually the result of that weeks- or=
 months-long process. It is now an art market convention that galleries ann=
ounce those pre-fair deals, and others, from the first day.

Since this newsletter will go out before the end of day two, here are the h=
ighlights from Thursday=E2=80=A6

* Almine Rech sold almost all of their Tomokazu Matsuyama paintings for pri=
ces from $100,000 to $600,000.
* Blum sold a Yoshitomo Nara sculpture for $750,000, as well as works by As=

Frieze week in L.A. has everything we love about the city itself=E2=80=94fr=
esh,
energetic works, attention-seeking hipsters, and poolside pop-ups. So what =
if it doesn=E2=80=99t always fit into a nice, neat art market narrative?

Marion Maneker ( https://puck.news/author/marion-maneker/?utm_campaign=3DWa=
ll+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&u=
tm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )
Marion Maneker ( https://puck.news/author/marion-maneker/?utm_campaign=3DWa=
ll+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRI=
BERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&u=
tm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a )

After a quiet winter in the art market, I headed out to Frieze week in Los =
Angeles not expecting
much. That=E2=80=99s mainly because we=E2=80=99ve had so little public evid=
ence of where the art market stands lately. Still, I was hoping Frieze L.A.=
 would provide at least a glimpse of the state of the market at the moment.=
 And it did, despite the lack of a trend to give the whole market coherence=
=2E

Of course, there=E2=80=99s a dark cloud hanging over the fair about the fat=
e of Frieze, itself, now that Endeavor has committed to selling it. Word is=
, the auction is proceeding slowly because, while the fair is
profitable, there=E2=80=99s not much prospect for growth. Also, Ari Emanuel=
 has already signaled ( https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-11-07/=
endeavor-s-emanuel-raises-funds-eyes-frieze-tennis-tournaments?utm_campaign=
=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SU=
BSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer=
=2Eio&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) an=
 interest in bidding, potentially sca=
ring off those only interested in a bargain.

Then there=E2=80=99s the uncertain future of the global economy: Convention=
al wisdom still views Trump as good for
business, but the administration=E2=80=99s sweeping plans to remake global =
trade and potentially roil the debt markets still loom. Don=E2=80=99t expec=
t the world=E2=80=99s wealthy to remain so sanguine if/when things end up i=
n a quagmire. Art buyers spend money they believe they can easily replace, =
and as soon as that sequence falters, art sales will slow even further. In =
brighter news, I did coax Mrs. Wallpower into joining me in L.A.=E2=80=94in=
 line with the old saying that if you want to travel fast, go alone; if you=
 want
to travel far, go together.

An art fair can often seem like a long trek up a steep mountain=E2=80=94the=
re are a lot of galleries to see. You=E2=80=99ve got to keep a pace, hit yo=
ur waypoints, and push through to the summit. It=E2=80=99s grueling. This i=
s particularly true in Los Angeles, where the venues are spread out across =
the city and traffic can push travel times up to 45 minutes or an hour, mak=
ing it logistically impossible to hit too many galleries without careful pl=
anning. Luckily, this was not our first
Frieze L.A., and our logistics game was on point. Still, I knew I would nee=
d to fortify my co-pilot and sherpa with a Hailey Bieber smoothie, so as so=
on as Mrs. Wallpower and I arrived in the city and dropped off our things, =
we headed straight to Erewhon for a liquid dinner.

Day One: Positive Signs
-----------------------

Tuesday started early. We fueled at Maxine=E2=80=99s with breakfast burrito=
s and honey buttered
biscuits, then headed downtown where, at Hauser & Wirth, Georges Rouy held =
forth on the second part of his very successful show of abstract paintings =
and explained his own take on figuration. He is continuing his show from Lo=
ndon=E2=80=99s Frieze week, last October, when Hauser raised his primary pr=
ices and sold out the studio. Hauser is a well-oiled marketing machine, and=
 their success was pretty much to be expected. So we needed to press on to =
find the real market story.

Pat Lipsky at James Fuentes, Frieze Los Angeles, 2025

Pat Lipsky at James Fuentes, Frieze Los Angeles, 2025

Next we headed to the clutch of galleries on North Western Avenue, where we=
 stopped at James Fuentes to see the octogenarian New York color field pain=
ter Pat Lipsky, whose work is accomplished and decorative. (Alas, promoting=
 once-overlooked women abstract painters isn=E2=80=99t as novel as it was a=
 few years ago.) Fuentes was just up the street from Lisa Yuskavage=E2=80=
=99s first L.A. show in 30 years, held at David Zwirner. Now an unquestione=
d presence in the
art firmament, Yuskavage took advantage of a press conference to hold forth=
 on the freedom afforded to her by so many years spent out of favor. Down t=
he block at Clearing, meanwhile, was a historical work by Larry Stanton, in=
cluding a short film of David Hockney making his famous paper pools series.

We ran into some friends outside Zwirner, so we asked them what was worth s=
eeing and were told: the Mike Kelley show. Advisors Vivian
Brodie and Alex Perweiler had collaborated with Hauser & Wirth, who represe=
nt the Mike Kelley Foundation, to feature Kelley=E2=80=99s disturbing re-cr=
eations of high school yearbook photographs. The show inaugurated the duo=
=E2=80=99s new space=E2=80=94with spectacular views, by the way=E2=80=94in =
a Wilshire Boulevard office building developed, so we were repeatedly told,=
 by pornographer Larry Flynt. A large statue of John Wayne on horseback sta=
nds out front. Brodie
and Perweiler are part of a trend on the secondary market where we=E2=80=99=
re seeing new ways to present art for sale. And anything new and even mildl=
y innovative is a sign of art market health.

Following that theme, we made our way high up into Beverly Hills to visit J=
ack Siebert, who was holding a portraiture show he called Mod=C3=A8le Vivan=
t=E2=80=94an opportunity for the twenty-something curator to show off his i=
nteresting taste in a setting aspirational enough to
justify the trip. Buck Ellison, the bard of preppy privilege, had created a=
n installation in a spare bedroom. In the living room, Siebert mounted work=
s by Violeta Maya, Olga de Amaral, Emmi Whitehorse, Joan Semmel, Chloe Wise=
, and many others, a surprising and appealing set of offerings. We left fee=
ling all art should be sold this way. After all, half of the joy in art is =
the story behind the
purchase.

From there, it made sense to drive down into Beverly Hills=E2=80=99s busine=
ss district to visit the Alex Israel show at Gagosian and Christie=E2=80=99=
s, where Darius Himes and Matthieu Humery were giving a talk on the Pinault=
 Collection=E2=80=99s interest in photography. We capped off the day with a=
 Persian dinner at Toranj in Westwood, where we took stock: We=E2=80=99d se=
en signs of a positive
mood, but nothing that overtly confirmed a real appetite in the market.

Day Two: The Gallery Scavenger Hunt
-----------------------------------

Wednesday=E2=80=99s main event was the Felix art fair at the Hollywood Roos=
evelt Hotel. The fair is
split into two sections=E2=80=94one poolside and the other in the hotel=E2=
=80=99s tower, where two floors are emptied of furniture and fairgoers sidl=
e past each other to enter and exit each hotel room. Actor Henry Winkler go=
t in early and eagerly investigated every one. This was something of the sw=
eet spot in the current market: a wide range of artists at affordable price=
s, and lots of ceramics. Down among the cabanas, we saw Adam Cohen selling =
100 small rondo portraits of
figures from art history in a hallway. At least one high-volume collector a=
nd dealer I know had expected Felix to be the week=E2=80=99s high point in =
terms of interest and energy, but when we left, at least, the pool area had=
 yet to develop into its usual raucous scene.

Gwyneth Paltrow at Frieze Los Angeles 2025.

Gwyneth Paltrow at Frieze Los Angeles 2025

There are more than a dozen galleries not far from the Roosevelt. So after =
a quick lunch, we headed to Jeffrey Deitch to see his Nina Chanel Abney sho=
w and stopped right next door at Lisson for Kelly
Akashi. Not being Angelenos, we walked West to Karma to see Woody De Othell=
o=E2=80=99s sculptures (all bathed in an otherworldly yellow light) and pai=
ntings hung around the perimeter of a room filled with sand. Further on, we=
 made it to Matthew Marks=E2=80=99s show of Jacqueline Humphries paintings.=
 Then we trucked it back to Deitch=E2=80=99s second space, filled with work=
 by Tschabalala Self, Regen Projects=E2=80=99s Doug Aitken show, and Tanya
Bonakdar=E2=80=99s Shilpa Gupta show, passing through the gallery Various S=
mall Fires before stopping in to see Terran Last Gun=E2=80=99s modern take =
on Indigenous ledger drawings and Julian Stanczak=E2=80=99s early work from=
 the =E2=80=9960s to the =E2=80=9980s at Diane Rosenstein=E2=80=99s gallery=
=2E

Diane herself took us in the back room to give us her insight on the consis=
tent trend of galleries selling ceramic works=E2=80=94as well as on how the=
 fires had devastated so many
on both sides of L.A.=E2=80=99s art equation. As Diane put it, she had been=
 hearing from collectors on the Westside who had lost work by her artists a=
nd wanted to let her know. Meanwhile, work from recent shows had headed wes=
t from the Eastside, back to artist=E2=80=99s studios, where some of it per=
ished. Diane was excited about her opening, but couldn=E2=80=99t quite say =
that everything was fine in the L.A. market for contemporary art.

We had dinner with some of my colleagues at Puck who were in town to do a
little business before heading to Silverlake to check in with the Basic.Spa=
ce boys, who were having a party for Frieze week at a local bar.

Unfrozen
--------

Finally, on Thursday morning, whatever doubts we had about the market were =
dispelled. A bright,
clear day had dawned, and inside the fair, the mood got even brighter. The =
aisles were bustling but not overcrowded. Some galleries brought a represen=
tative selection from their stable of artists; others showed off discoverie=
s like Greta Sch=C3=B6dl, a 95-year-old still working in text, at Richard S=
altoun. Karma had work by Norman Zammitt showing nearby more ( https://puck=
=2Enews/inside-the-growing-market-for-g=
ertrude-abercrombies-art/?utm_campaign=
=3DWall+Power+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_content=3DWall+Power+-+SU=
BSCRIBERS+%282%2F21%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demail_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer=
=2Eio&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ) Ge=
rtrude Abercrombie works, including a=
 tiny Abercrombie in the booth=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Cback room.=E2=80=9D Almin=
e Rech had a showstopping booth of work by Tomokazu Matsuyama, including on=
e that had been bought by the Centre Pompidou and another by a museum in Ja=
pan. Artist Rebecca Ness bravely toured the fair, helping her dealer, Jessi=
ca Silverman, make a sale.

Tomokazu Matsuyama at Almine Rech, Frieze Los Angeles, 2025.

Tomokazu Matsuyama at Almine Rech, Frieze Los Angeles, 2025

There were even some big names in attendance,
including former Merrill Lynch C.E.O. John Thain. I ran into collectors Don=
 Kaul and Barbara Bluhm-Kaul talking up their support for a new event in As=
pen. And on my way out of the fair, I flagged down Puck superfan Katie Cour=
ic making the rounds. We even encountered that big collector who had predic=
ted all the energy would be at Felix. He had decided to make the trip at th=
e last minute and was happily surprised to be proven
wrong by how vibrant Frieze was this year.

Escaping the fair, we stopped at Ghisallo for a late lunch in the garden re=
fuge and took stock once more. Throughout the fair, the mood was carefree a=
nd surprisingly focused on the art, but the current art market lacks a narr=
ative. There=E2=80=99s no central driver of the story that situates the ind=
ividual artists into a broader theme. There are rediscovered artists, and h=
istorical works, and artists with novel life stories, but no real tie among=
 all of
these. That can make the art market seem directionless or lacking in excite=
ment.

In the usual art cycle, right now collectors would be going back to recent =
art history, buying solid names with strong market track records. I did see=
 a very nice 1999 work by John Currin at Zwirner=E2=80=99s booth, and Glads=
tone had a good portion of their booth devoted to Keith Haring, which seems=
 to have sold well. But those are just two names of what should be many mor=
e. Maybe
that will come, but an extended season of chaos in D.C. and beyond won=E2=
=80=99t help get us there.

Notably, what we did not see or hear at Frieze was grousing or complaints. =
A lack of excitement isn=E2=80=99t the same as a gloom or dejection. These =
days, that may be the only victory we can hope for.

50%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;">Aft=
er a quiet winter in the art market, I headed out to Frieze week in Los Ang=
eles not expecting
much. That=E2=80=99s mainly because we=E2=80=99ve had so little public evid=
ence of where the art market stands lately. Still, I was hoping Frieze L.A.=
 would provide at least a glimpse of the state of the market at the moment.=
 And it did, despite the lack of a trend to give the whole market coherence=
=2E<br/><br/>Of course, there=E2=80=99s a=20=
dark cloud hanging over the fair ab=
out the fate of Frieze, itself, now that Endeavor has committed to selling =
it. Word is, the auction is proceeding slowly because, while the fair is
profitable, there=E2=80=99s not much prospect for growth. Also, <strong>Ari=
 Emanuel</strong> has already <a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbW=
FpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dWS3RrYjV4YXR6ZmVRODNXZnFYZz0iLCJocmVmI=

UyOVx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PVdhbGwrUG93ZXIrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgyJTJGMjElM=

e8eec9e5dd354863a6cd5346862adedc459061" style=3D"text-decoration:underline;=
color:#d40000;">signaled</a> an interest in bidding, potentially scaring of=
f those only interested in a bargain.<br/><br/>Then there=E2=80=99s the unc=
ertain future of the global economy: Conventional wisdom still views <stron=
g>Trump</strong> as good for
business, but the administration=E2=80=99s sweeping plans to remake global =
trade and potentially roil the debt markets still loom. Don=E2=80=99t expec=
t the world=E2=80=99s wealthy to remain so sanguine if/when things end up i=
n a quagmire. Art buyers spend money they believe they can easily replace, =
and as soon as that sequence falters, art sales will slow even further. In =
brighter news, I did coax Mrs. Wallpower into joining me in L.A.=E2=80=94in=
 line with the old saying that if you want to travel fast, go alone; if you=
 want
to travel far, go together.<br/><br/>An art fair can often seem like a long=
 trek up a steep mountain=E2=80=94there are a lot of galleries to see. You=
=E2=80=99ve got to keep a pace, hit your waypoints, and push through to the=
 summit. It=E2=80=99s grueling. This is particularly true in Los Angeles, w=
here the venues are spread out across the city and traffic can push travel =
times up to 45 minutes or an hour, making it logistically impossible to hit=
 too many galleries without careful planning. Luckily, this was not our fir=
st
Frieze L.A., and our logistics game was on point. Still, I knew I would nee=
d to fortify my co-pilot and sherpa with a <strong>Hailey Bieber </strong>s=
moothie, so as soon as Mrs. Wallpower and I arrived in the city and dropped=
 off our things, we headed straight to Erewhon for a liquid dinner.</p></di=

n-bottom:0px;">Next we headed to the clutch of galleries on North Western A=
venue, where we stopped at James Fuentes to see the octogenarian New York c=
olor field painter <strong>Pat Lipsky</strong>, whose work is accomplished =
and decorative. (Alas, promoting once-overlooked women abstract painters is=
n=E2=80=99t as novel as it was a few years ago.) Fuentes was just up the st=
reet from <strong>Lisa Yuskavage</strong>=E2=80=99s first L.A. show in 30 y=
ears, held at David Zwirner. Now an unquestioned presence in the
art firmament, Yuskavage took advantage of a press conference to hold forth=
 on the freedom afforded to her by so many years spent out of favor. Down t=
he block at Clearing, meanwhile, was a historical work by <strong>Larry Sta=
nton</strong>, including a short film of <strong>David Hockney</strong> mak=
ing his famous paper pools series.<br/><br/>We ran into some friends outsid=
e Zwirner, so we asked them what was worth seeing and were told: the <stron=
g>Mike Kelley</strong> show. Advisors <strong>Vivian
Brodie</strong> and <strong>Alex Perweiler</strong> had collaborated with H=
auser & Wirth, who represent the Mike Kelley Foundation, to feature Kel=
ley=E2=80=99s disturbing re-creations of high school yearbook photographs. =
The show inaugurated the duo=E2=80=99s new space=E2=80=94with spectacular v=
iews, by the way=E2=80=94in a Wilshire Boulevard office building developed,=
 so we were repeatedly told, by pornographer <strong>Larry Flynt</strong>. =
A large statue of <strong>John Wayne</strong> on horseback stands out front=
=2E Brodie
and Perweiler are part of a trend on the secondary market where we=E2=80=99=
re seeing new ways to present art for sale. And anything new and even mildl=
y innovative is a sign of art market health.<br/><br/>Following that theme,=
 we made our way high up into Beverly Hills to visit <strong>Jack Siebert</=
strong>, who was holding a portraiture show he called <em>Mod=C3=A8le Vivan=
t</em>=E2=80=94an opportunity for the twenty-something curator to show off =
his interesting taste in a setting aspirational enough to
justify the trip. <strong>Buck Ellison</strong>, the bard of preppy privile=
ge, had created an installation in a spare bedroom. In the living room, Sie=
bert mounted works by <strong>Violeta Maya</strong>, <strong>Olga de Amaral=
</strong>, <strong>Emmi Whitehorse</strong>, <strong>Joan Semmel</strong>, =
<strong>Chloe Wise</strong>, and many others, a surprising and appealing se=
t of offerings. We left feeling all art should be sold this way. After all,=
 half of the joy in art is the story behind the
purchase.<br/><br/>From there, it made sense to drive down into Beverly Hil=
ls=E2=80=99s business district to visit the <strong>Alex Israel</strong> sh=
ow at Gagosian and Christie=E2=80=99s, where <strong>Darius Himes</strong> =
and <strong>Matthieu Humery</strong> were giving a talk on the Pinault Coll=
ection=E2=80=99s interest in photography. We capped off the day with a Pers=
ian dinner at Toranj in Westwood, where we took stock: We=E2=80=99d seen si=
gns of a positive
mood, but nothing that overtly confirmed a real appetite in the market.</p>=

 =E2=80=9980s at <strong>Diane Rosenstein</strong>=E2=80=99s gallery.<br/><=
br/>Diane herself took us in the back room to give us her insight on the co=

 the fires had devastated so many

 hearing from collectors on the Westside who had lost work by her artists a=

re were even some big names in attendance,
including former Merrill Lynch C.E.O. <strong>John Thain</strong>. I ran in=
to collectors <strong>Don Kaul</strong> and <strong>Barbara Bluhm-Kaul</str=
ong> talking up their support for a new event in Aspen. And on my way out o=
f the fair, I flagged down Puck superfan <strong>Katie Couric</strong> maki=
ng the rounds. We even encountered that big collector who had predicted all=
 the energy would be at Felix. He had decided to make the trip at the last =
minute and was happily surprised to be proven
wrong by how vibrant Frieze was this year.<br/><br/>Escaping the fair, we s=
topped at Ghisallo for a late lunch in the garden refuge and took stock onc=
e more. Throughout the fair, the mood was carefree and surprisingly focused=
 on the art, but the current art market lacks a narrative. There=E2=80=99s =
no central driver of the story that situates the individual artists into a =
broader theme. There are rediscovered artists, and historical works, and ar=
tists with novel life stories, but no real tie among all of
these. That can make the art market seem directionless or lacking in excite=
ment.<br/><br/>In the usual art cycle, right now collectors would be going =
back to recent art history, buying solid names with strong market track rec=
ords. I did see a very nice 1999 work by <strong>John Currin</strong> at Zw=
irner=E2=80=99s booth, and Gladstone had a good portion of their booth devo=
ted to <strong>Keith Haring</strong>, which seems to have sold well. But th=
ose are just two names of what should be many more. Maybe
that will come, but an extended season of chaos in D.C. and beyond won=E2=
=80=99t help get us there.<br/><br/>Notably, what we did not see or hear at=
 Frieze was grousing or complaints. A lack of excitement isn=E2=80=99t the =
same as a gloom or dejection. These days, that may be the only victory we c=
an hope for.</p></div>

ave felt she needed to explain herself.<br/><br/>For both Sotheby=E2=80=99s=

fit of a relationship with the family. If Christie=E2=80=99s did indeed off=
er a lower upfront guarantee, the Riggios=E2=80=99 legal advisors surely st=
ructured the
